title: Tree Traversal
summary: Navigate hierarchical data structures — from DOM trees to nested menus — using depth-first or breadth-first strategies.
overview: Tree traversal visits every node in a tree exactly once. Depth-first search (DFS) goes deep before wide: pre-order, in-order, post-order variants each have distinct uses. Breadth-first search (BFS) visits level by level using a queue. Frontend engineers encounter trees constantly: the DOM, component trees, file systems, comment threads, navigation menus.
coreConcept: A tree node has a value and zero or more children. DFS uses the call stack (or explicit stack) to go deep; BFS uses a queue to go wide. Pre-order (visit before children) is natural for rendering. Post-order (visit after children) is natural for cleanup. In-order (left, node, right) is specific to binary search trees and gives sorted output.
problemPatterns: Find a node by value or condition
Compute depth / height of a tree
Find path from root to a node
signals: You need to visit every node
You're building breadcrumbs or ancestry paths

frontendScenarios.title: Nested Comment Thread Rendering
frontendScenarios.description: Reddit-style comments have replies that have replies. The data is a tree. Rendering requires DFS pre-order: render a comment, then recursively render its replies, indented one level deeper.

frontendScenarios.title: File System Explorer
frontendScenarios.description: A folder tree needs expand/collapse. BFS is useful for loading level by level (lazy loading). DFS is useful for building the full path to a file or finding all files matching a pattern.

frontendScenarios.title: Navigation Menu Active State
frontendScenarios.description: A multi-level nav menu needs to highlight the active path. Given the current route, traverse the menu tree to find which items are ancestors of the active item.

dataThinking: Think of tree traversal as a controlled visit schedule. The shape of your visit order encodes what information you have available at each step. Pre-order: you know the parent before children (useful for passing props down). Post-order: you know all children's results before computing parent (useful for aggregation). BFS: you know everyone at depth d before depth d+1 (useful for level-based operations).

naiveApproach.description: Ad-hoc recursion without clear traversal strategy. Often results in incorrect visit order or stack overflows on deep trees.

betterApproach.description: Choose traversal strategy intentionally. Use recursive DFS for most tree problems (clean code, natural for trees). Use iterative DFS (explicit stack) to avoid stack overflow on very deep trees. Use BFS (queue) for level-order needs.
betterApproach.complexity: O(n) time, O(h) space for DFS (h=height), O(w) space for BFS (w=max width)

visualizationSteps.label: Tree structure
visualizationSteps.explanation: A has children B, C. B has children D, E. C has child F. Starting DFS pre-order from A.

visualizationSteps.label: Visit A, recurse to B
visualizationSteps.explanation: Visit A first (pre-order). Push children right-to-left on stack: C, then B. Pop B next.

visualizationSteps.label: Visit B, D, E (subtree)
visualizationSteps.explanation: Visit B, push E then D. Pop D (leaf, no children), visit D. Pop E (leaf), visit E. B's subtree is done.

visualizationSteps.label: Visit C, F — complete
visualizationSteps.explanation: Pop C, visit it, push F. Pop F (leaf), visit F. Stack empty — traversal complete. Pre-order: A B D E C F.

practiceItems.title: Maximum Depth of Nested Menu
practiceItems.question: Given a navigation tree where each item may have children, find the maximum nesting depth. This tells you how deep the deepest submenu goes — useful for dynamic indent calculations.
practiceItems.hint1: Post-order recursion: compute depth of each child, take the max, add 1 for current node.
practiceItems.hint2: Base case: a leaf node (no children) has depth 1.

practiceItems.title: Flatten Nested Category Tree
practiceItems.question: An e-commerce API returns categories as a tree. You need a flat sorted list for a dropdown select. Each item should show its full path (e.g. 'Electronics > Phones > Android').
practiceItems.hint1: DFS pre-order, passing the current path string down. When you reach any node, add its full path to the result.
practiceItems.hint2: You can include both leaf and non-leaf nodes — each gets its accumulated path.

practiceItems.title: Find Breadcrumb Path
practiceItems.question: Given a page's URL slug and a site navigation tree, return the array of nav items from root to the matching item (the breadcrumb trail).
practiceItems.hint1: DFS with path accumulation. Pass the current path down; return it when you find a match.
practiceItems.hint2: Return null from each branch that doesn't contain the target. Bubble up the successful path.

reflectionPrompts: When would you choose BFS over DFS for a frontend problem? Give a concrete example.
React's component tree is traversed during reconciliation. How does React's algorithm relate to tree traversal concepts?
What are the risks of deep recursion in JavaScript, and how would you convert a recursive DFS to an iterative one?
How does post-order traversal map to React's useEffect cleanup ordering?
